title: Footed Charcuterie Board
summary: Four cheap 1x3s, a handle, and two feet — pieced together instead of cut from one expensive slab.
description:
A charcuterie board is usually one expensive slab of edge-grain hardwood — or it can be four cheap 1x3s and a handle, glued edge to edge and set on two feet. Nobody at the table can tell the difference once it's oiled and loaded with cheese.

The **feet are not decorative.** Without them, a flat board sweats condensation onto the table underneath it, and the underside never gets air between uses. Two glued-on strips solve both problems for the cost of a scrap offcut.

The handle is the only piece that extends past the others — that overhang is what makes it liftable as a tray, not just a board you slide around the counter.

tools.note: A circular saw with a guide works too.
Speeds up attaching the feet. Clamps and glue alone work fine.
Only needed if you round the corners.
For the edge-to-edge glue-up. This is the joint that actually holds the board together.

materials.name: Poplar or pine, 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Any species works — this is a piecing technique, not a wood-selection showcase.

materials.name: Poplar or pine, 1x2, 5 ft
materials.note: The handle and the two feet come from this.

materials.name: Food-safe butcher block finish (mineral oil or board butter)
materials.note: Never use a film finish like polyurethane on something food touches.

cutList.note: Extends past the main boards — this is what makes it a handle.

steps.title: Cut everything to length
steps.body:
Cut the four main boards to 23-3/4" and the handle piece to 28-1/2". The handle is deliberately longer than the main boards — that extra length is what you'll grip when the board is loaded with food and you need to carry it one-handed.

Cut the two foot pieces to 11-1/2" now too, even though they don't get glued on until the end.

steps.title: Glue up the boards edge to edge
steps.body:
Lay the four main boards side by side, alternating end grain if you can see it, and run a bead of glue down each mating edge. Add the handle piece to one side, flush at one end and overhanging at the other.

Clamp across the width, not just at the ends — a glue-up this wide wants to bow if you only clamp the outside edges. Wipe squeeze-out before it skins over; dried glue won't take stain or oil evenly.

steps.title: Attach the feet
steps.body:
Once the glue-up is dry, glue and nail the two foot pieces to the underside, running perpendicular to the main boards, a few inches in from each end.

This is the whole reason the board doesn't sweat: it lifts the underside off the counter so air actually reaches both faces between uses.

steps.title: Round the corners and sand
steps.body:
Optional: trace a curve at each corner with anything round you have on hand and cut it with a jigsaw for a softer look.

Sand through the grits, finishing no coarser than 220 — anything rougher will feel splintery under a block of cheese and a knife.

steps.title: Finish food-safe
steps.body:
Flood the board with mineral oil or a board butter (beeswax and oil), let it soak in, and wipe off the excess. Do the underside and the feet too, not just the top — end grain and fresh-cut edges drink finish fastest and are the first places a board dries out and cracks.

Re-oil every few weeks at first, then whenever the surface starts looking dry.
materials: Food-safe butcher block finish (mineral oil or board butter)
